questions.question: What is the key difference between coaching and therapy?

options.text: They are the same thing
Coaching focuses on goals/performance improvement in non-clinical contexts; therapy diagnoses and treats mental health conditions
Coaching is more effective than therapy
Only coaches can ask powerful questions

questions.explanation: Coaching focuses on goal achievement and performance improvement for people who are functioning (non-clinical). Therapy diagnoses and treats clinical mental health conditions. Coaches help clients find their own answers through skilled questioning; therapists provide evidence-based treatments for pathology. Different roles for different needs. The key is knowing which is appropriate when.

questions.question: What makes a question 'powerful' in coaching?

options.text: It contains the answer you think they should find
It's open-ended, client-centered, non-leading, and invites reflection
It's as long and complex as possible
It starts with 'Why don't you just...?'

questions.explanation: Powerful questions are: open-ended (can't be answered yes/no), client-centered (about their experience), non-leading (doesn't contain the 'right' answer), and invite reflection/exploration. Examples: 'What matters most to you about this?' 'What would success look like?' They facilitate insight rather than providing answers. Leading questions like 'Don't you think you should...?' contain your answer, not space for theirs.

questions.question: What are SMART goals and why do they matter for coaching?

options.text: Smart people's goals
Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound—clear goals increase success rates
Goals that sound impressive
SMART is outdated; vague goals work better

questions.explanation: SMART stands for: Specific (clear, concrete), Measurable (you'll know when accomplished), Achievable (realistic given resources), Relevant (aligns with values/priorities), Time-bound (has deadline). Research shows specific goals dramatically increase success rates versus vague goals. Example: 'Walk 20 minutes 3x/week for next month' (SMART) vs. 'Be healthier' (vague). Coach role is helping articulate SMART goals, not imposing your goals.

questions.question: When should you refer someone to professional help instead of coaching/mentoring?

options.text: Never; coaching can handle everything
Clinical symptoms (depression, anxiety, trauma), substance abuse, self-harm/suicidal thoughts, abuse situations, severe stress impairing function
Only if they explicitly ask for therapy
Coaching and therapy are interchangeable

questions.explanation: Red flags requiring professional referral: clinical symptoms (depression, anxiety, PTSD), substance abuse interfering with functioning, self-harm or suicidal thoughts, relationship violence/abuse, eating disorders, severe stress impairing daily function, legal/medical issues outside your expertise. What to say: 'I care about you and want to support you, but what you're describing sounds like something a therapist could help with more effectively. I'm happy to help you find that support.' Stay in your lane.

questions.question: What unique value does mentoring add beyond coaching skills?

options.text: Mentoring is the same as coaching
Lived experience ('I've been there'), insider knowledge, advocacy, perspective, role modeling
Mentors never use powerful questions
Mentoring is just giving advice

questions.explanation: Mentors offer: lived experience ('When I was navigating X, here's what I learned'), insider knowledge (how systems actually work, unwritten rules), advocacy (opening doors, making introductions), perspective ('This challenge is normal at this stage'), and role modeling (demonstrating what success/recovery looks like). Best mentors COMBINE this with coaching skills (powerful questions, active listening), not just advice-giving. The shared experience creates credibility and hope.
